title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR has several blockers that must be fixed before merge. The most critical is a PCI-DSS violation: the full charge request body (including card number and CVC) is written to stdout via console.log. There is also a correctness bug that makes discount_amount always 0, a swallowed exception that silently eats coupon failures, and zero unit tests for money-adjacent arithmetic. On top of those blockers, the new couponDiscount.ts file introduces a needlessly heavy Strategy+Factory abstraction over a plain lookup table, an abstract class that should be an interface, pervasive bad naming (doStuff, theData, tmp, result2, x), var/== convention drift, snake_case fields on what is otherwise a camelCase API, and two more debug console.log calls that bypass the project's pino logger.

findings.title: Full charge body (PAN + CVC) logged to stdout — PCI-DSS violation
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serializes the entire request body, which contains the raw card number and CVC, and writes it to stdout on every single charge request. PCI-DSS explicitly forbids storing or logging CVC anywhere and requires PAN to be masked/tokenized. The project already ships a pino logger with a redaction allowlist for exactly this reason; this `console.log` bypasses that control entirely. Delete this line. If you need observability, log only `{ customerId, currency, couponCode }` via the injected `logger.debug()`.

findings.title: `as any` on transaction object before JSON serialization — uncontrolled data leak
findings.message: `const response: any = transaction` strips all TypeScript type safety from the transaction object and then passes it straight to `res.json()`. If `transaction` carries any fields beyond what the client should see (internal IDs, gateway tokens, card hashes, etc.), they all go to the wire. Model the response shape with an explicit interface or a pick/omit, then build `{ ...pickedFields, discountAmount, couponCode }`. Never use `as any` as a shortcut to attach extra fields on an object you're about to serialize to untrusted callers.

findings.title: `couponCode` read from raw body before schema validation and echoed in response
findings.message: `couponCode` is plucked from `req.body` *before* `chargeSchema.parse()`, so it never passes through Zod. It's then echoed verbatim back in the response at line 37. An attacker can send an arbitrarily long or specially crafted string as `couponCode`, which gets logged (line 61 in couponDiscount.ts) and returned in the response with zero sanitisation. Move `couponCode` inside the Zod schema with `z.string().min(1).max(32).toUpperCase().optional()` and read it from the validated `payload`.

findings.title: Swallowed exception silently charges full price on any coupon error
findings.message: `catch (e) { // TODO }` drops every exception from `doStuff` on the floor. If the coupon engine throws for any reason — bad input, a future bug in a new strategy — the charge silently proceeds at full price, `discount_amount` stays 0, and there is no log, no metric, and no way for the caller to know what happened. Either handle the error meaningfully (e.g., return a 422 for an invalid coupon) or at minimum rethrow so the outer handler can log and respond correctly. A TODO comment in a catch block is a silent data-correctness bug waiting to happen.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 — `payload.amount` was overwritten before the diff
findings.message: At line 24, `payload.amount = finalAmount` overwrites the original amount with the discounted amount. Then at line 36, `payload.amount - finalAmount` computes `finalAmount - finalAmount`, which is always 0. The client will never see the actual discount. Fix: save the original amount before the try block — `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` — and compute `response.discountAmount = originalAmount - finalAmount`.

findings.title: Zero unit tests for money arithmetic — PR author flagged this as TODO
findings.message: There are no unit tests for any of the coupon math, and the PR description explicitly marks them as a follow-up TODO. For money-adjacent code this is a blocker: `PercentageCouponStrategy.apply()` uses `Math.floor()` (a truncation decision that affects real cents), `FixedCouponStrategy` does no underflow guard (a $10 coupon on a $3 charge returns a negative amount), and `doStuff` has at least two branches worth covering (unknown code, no code, each coupon type). Ship the tests with the feature, not after.

findings.title: Strategy + Factory + abstract class for a 6-row lookup table — pure over-engineering
Abstract class with one abstract method and no shared behavior — use an interface
findings.message: `AbstractCouponStrategy` has a single `abstract apply()` method and zero concrete methods or shared state. That is the definition of an interface. An abstract class earns its keep when it provides concrete shared logic; this one just forces single inheritance for no benefit. Replace it with `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }`. (And then delete it once you've replaced the whole thing with a lookup table.)

findings.title: Exported entry point named `doStuff` — tells callers nothing
findings.message: `doStuff` is the public API that every caller imports. It describes neither its input domain nor its effect. Name it for what it does: `applyCoupon(amount: number, couponCode?: string): number`. The same problem runs through the whole file: `theData` (line 15 in the controller), `tmp` (line 59), `result2` (line 64), `x` (line 17), `p` (line 11), `amt` (line 23). Names are the cheapest documentation — use them.

findings.title: `console.log` instead of injected pino logger
findings.message: The project uses a pino logger injected through the service layer. Stray `console.log` calls bypass log levels, structured fields, and — critically — the pino redaction allowlist that keeps PAN out of logs. Both debug lines (`paymentsController.ts:17` and `couponDiscount.ts:61`) should either be deleted or replaced with `logger.debug({ couponCode }, 'applied coupon')` via the injected logger. There should be no bare `console.*` calls in a finished diff.

findings.title: snake_case response fields on a camelCase API (`discount_amount`, `coupon_code`)
findings.message: The rest of the API uses camelCase in its JSON responses (confirmed by the existing `chargeSchema` and transaction shape). Adding `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` here breaks that contract and forces every consumer to special-case this endpoint. Rename to `discountAmount` and `couponCode`. (This is also a likely sort/filter footgun: any client code that references these fields by the camelCase name will silently get `undefined`.)

findings.title: `var` and `==` instead of `const`/`let` and `===` throughout the new file
findings.message: `var x = amount * (1 - this.p)` (line 17) and `var tmp = ...` (line 59) use `var`, which the rest of the codebase does not. The factory uses `==` for all string comparisons (lines 35, 37, 39, 41, 43, 45) and `tmp == null` (line 60) instead of `===`. `prefer-const` and `eqeqeq` are standard ESLint rules for a reason: `var` has function scope and `==` coerces. Use `const`/`let` and `===` throughout.

findings.title: Commented-out `FREESHIP` code should be deleted
findings.message: The commented-out `else if (code == 'FREESHIP')` block is dead code with a note saying 'shipping not used yet.' Git remembers history — there's no reason to embalm it in the source. Delete it. Future readers shouldn't have to decide whether it's a plan, a rollback, or a fossil.
